LAW AND MOTION, 302, NO APPEARANCE ON THIS MATTER. RULING: THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANTS WALGREEN CO. AND JORGE BLANCO'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT IS OVERRULED. MS. SHANKS HAS PLED SUFFICIENT FACTS TO ALLEGE THE SEVENTH AND EIGHTH CAUSES OF ACTION, SPECIFICALLY, THAT DEFENDANTS' MOTIVATING REASON FOR THEIR CONDUCT WAS THEIR PERCEPTION OF PLAINTIFF'S RACE. (CACI NOS. 3060, 3063. SEE SECOND AM. COMPLAINT, 12, 26, 71, 80.) THE PLEADINGS ARE NOT UNCERTAIN. (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER.) JUDGE: MARLA J. MILLER; CLERK: LESLEY FISCELLA, COURT REPORTER: ANTHONY VAUGHN, CSR #6185

Off Calendar Feb-27-2015 Case Settled - Set For Crc 3.1385 Hrg On May-05-15 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication And Index Of Evidence
Off Calendar Jan-30-2015 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. LYNDON Y. CHEE of Geonetta & Frucht, LLP, Tel No. (415) 433-4589 appearing for Plaintiff Cheryl Shanks;
Atty. PATRICK M. CALLAHAN of Burnham & Brown, Tel No. (510) 444-6800 appearing for Defendants Walgreen Co., Jorge Blanco, and Alex Ortega.

RULING - A R G U E D - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANTS WALGREEN CO., JORGE BLANCO, AND ALEX ORTEGA'S Motion for summary adjudication is granted. As to the Violation of the Unruh Civil Rights Act and Ralph Civil Rights Act causes of action, Defendants met their initial burden of presenting evidence that the employees' conduct was not motivated by race. The evidence submitted by Defendants establishes that the employees only began to watch Plaintiff after another employee witnessed Plaintiff put merchandise in her bag and the employees only attempted to restrain Plaintiff until she began to act aggressively. (Defendant's Undisputed Material Facts ("UMF") 4-6, 13-16.) Plaintiff does not submit evidence raising a triable issue of material fact that Defendants' conduct was motivated by race. Plaintiff's testimony that she spoke with other unidentified African-American friends and acquaintances who told her they felt they were unfairly watched by Walgreens employees is insufficient to raise a triable issue of material fact, as it is speculative and based on hearsay. As to the punitive damages claim, Defendants met their initial burden by presenting evidence showing that Defendants Blanco and Ortega's conduct is protected by the Merchant's Privilege, as set forth in Cal. Penal Code sec. 490.5(f). (UMF 4-6, 13-16.) Plaintiff does not present clear and convincing evidence raising a triable issue of material fact that Defendants Blanco and Ortega did not have probable cause to suspect Plaintiff of shoplifting. Additionally, Plaintiff does not present clear and convincing evidence raising a triable issue of material fact as to whether Defendants Blanco and Ortega's conduct went beyond "a reasonable amount of nondeadly force necessary to protect [themselves] and to prevent escape of the person detained or the loss of tangible or intangible property." (Cal. Penal Code sec. 490.5(f)(2).) The Court sustains Defendants' objection to the surveillance video. The surveillance video was not properly authenticated by an individual with personal knowledge of the incident. (Cal. Evid. Code secs. 702(a), 1400, 1401.) COURT SHALL REVIEW THE PROPOSED ORDER SUBMITTED BY DEFENSE COUNSEL AND MAIL THE ORDER TO PARTIES ONCE SIGNED. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Melanie Dawn Gheno, CSR #7489 (302/EHG)
